source: UC Admissions PDFs in Downloads/UC Transfer Chatbot/IGetc-CalGetc

programs.name: IGETC
programs.summary: California community college students can complete IGETC to satisfy most freshman/sophomore-level general education requirements before transferring to UC.
programs.availability: Available to students who enrolled at a California community college before fall 2025. As of fall 2025, Cal-GETC is the only pattern available for newly enrolling students. UC will continue to accept IGETC certifications for the foreseeable future.

requirements.name: English Communication
requirements.required_courses: One course in English composition and one course in critical thinking/English composition.

requirements.name: Arts and Humanities
requirements.required_courses: Three courses with at least one from the arts and one from the humanities.

requirements.name: Social and Behavioral Sciences
requirements.required_courses: Two courses from at least two disciplines or in an interdisciplinary sequence.

requirements.name: Physical and Biological Sciences
requirements.required_courses: One physical science course and one biological science course, with at least one laboratory.

requirements.name: Language Other than English
requirements.required_courses: Proficiency equivalent to two years of high school courses in the same language.

notes.text: IGETC for STEM is only available to students earning an associate degree for transfer at a California community college in a major that offers IGETC for STEM as an option.
Courses count toward IGETC with grades of C or better. Credit or Pass can count if the community college treats it as equivalent to C or better.
Specific AP exam scores of 3, 4, or 5 may fulfill some IGETC requirements.
An English AP score of 3, 4, or 5 may meet English Composition Area 1A, but not Critical Thinking/English Composition Area 1B.
IGETC courses can be taken at more than one California community college, but coursework only applies in the area where it was approved at the college where it was completed.
Some community colleges may grant partial IGETC certification when no more than two requirements are missing.
Certification is the responsibility of the last California community college attended. Students should verify that their UC school, college, or major accepts partial certification.

programs.name: Cal-GETC
programs.summary: California community college students can complete Cal-GETC to satisfy most freshman/sophomore-level general education requirements before transferring to UC.
programs.availability: Cal-GETC is the new general education pattern at all California community colleges. If a student enrolled at a California community college in fall 2025, this is the available pattern.

requirements.name: English Communication
requirements.required_courses: One course in English composition, one course in critical thinking and composition, and one course in oral communication.

requirements.name: Arts and Humanities
requirements.required_courses: Two courses with at least one from the arts and one from the humanities.

requirements.name: Social and Behavioral Sciences
requirements.required_courses: Two courses from at least two disciplines.

requirements.name: Physical and Biological Sciences
requirements.required_courses: At least two courses: one physical science course and one biological science course, with at least one laboratory.

notes.text: Courses count toward Cal-GETC with grades of C or better. Credit or Pass can count if the community college treats it as equivalent to C or better.
Specific AP exam scores of 3, 4, or 5 may fulfill some Cal-GETC requirements.
An English AP score of 3, 4, or 5 may meet English Composition Area 1A, but not Critical Thinking and Composition Area 1B.
Cal-GETC courses can be taken at more than one California community college, but coursework only applies in the area where it was approved at the college where it was completed.

Cal-GETC is not a UC admission requirement, but some campuses may prefer completion for selection. If Cal-GETC is incomplete, completed courses receive credit as appropriate for the UC's general education breadth requirements.
